name: Arrays
description: A contiguous block of memory storing elements of the same type, accessible via zero-based integer indices in O(1) time.
intuition: Think of an array as a row of numbered mailboxes: each box holds exactly one item, every box has a fixed address, and you can jump directly to any box without opening others.
analogy: A cinema row where every seat is numbered from 0. You can go directly to seat 7 without passing through the others—but adding a new seat in the middle requires shifting everyone.
common_misconceptions: Arrays are dynamic by default—they are not; most languages require pre-allocation or use wrappers (ArrayList, vector) for resizing.
Inserting at the beginning is cheap—it is O(n) because every subsequent element must shift.
Arrays and linked lists have identical performance—random access in arrays is O(1) vs O(n) in linked lists.
key_takeaways: O(1) random access via index.
O(n) insertion/deletion in the middle.
Cache-friendly due to memory contiguity.
Fixed size in static arrays; dynamic arrays amortise resize cost to O(1) amortised append.
learning_objectives: Explain how contiguous memory enables O(1) index access.
Identify the time complexity of common array operations: access, search, insert, delete.
Apply two-pointer and sliding-window patterns on arrays.
Distinguish when to use an array versus a linked list.

practice_examples.question: Given an array [2, 7, 11, 15] and target 9, find two indices whose values sum to the target.
practice_examples.answer: Indices 0 and 1 (values 2 + 7 = 9). Use a hash map for O(n): for each element x, check if (target - x) already exists in the map.

practice_examples.question: Rotate the array [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] to the right by k=2 positions in-place with O(1) extra space.
practice_examples.answer: Reverse the whole array → [5,4,3,2,1]. Reverse first k elements → [4,5,3,2,1]. Reverse remaining n-k elements → [4,5,1,2,3]. Three-reversal trick completes in O(n) time.

practice_examples.question: What is the time complexity of deleting an element at index i from an array of n elements, and why?
practice_examples.answer: O(n) in the worst case. Every element after index i must shift one position left to fill the gap, requiring up to n-1 moves.

name: Strings
description: An ordered sequence of characters, typically immutable, stored as a character array and manipulated via slicing, searching, and pattern-matching operations.
intuition: A string is an array of characters with extra semantics—encoding, immutability guarantees, and a rich library of operations built on top of that underlying array.
analogy: A printed sentence on a strip of tape. You can read any letter by position, but cutting and re-splicing (concatenation) creates a brand new strip every time.
common_misconceptions: String concatenation inside a loop is O(n)—in languages with immutable strings each '+' allocates a new object, making naive loops O(n²).
All string comparisons are O(1)—they are O(k) where k is the length of the compared prefix.
Characters are always single bytes—Unicode code points can be multi-byte; length may differ from character count.
key_takeaways: Treat strings as immutable arrays of characters unless the language guarantees otherwise.
Use StringBuilder/join patterns to avoid O(n²) concatenation.
Key operations: substring O(k), search O(n·m) naïve or O(n+m) with KMP/Rabin-Karp.
Hashing strings enables O(1) average equality checks and is the backbone of rolling-hash techniques.
learning_objectives: Explain why naive string concatenation in a loop is O(n²).
Apply sliding window to find substrings satisfying a constraint.
Use a frequency map to detect anagrams and permutations.
Identify when rolling hash or two-pointer reduces a string problem from O(n²) to O(n).

practice_examples.question: Determine whether the string 'anagram' is an anagram of 'nagaram'.
practice_examples.answer: Yes. Sort both strings and compare, or count character frequencies in O(n) using a 26-element array. Both yield equal distributions.

practice_examples.question: Find the length of the longest substring without repeating characters in 'abcabcbb'.
practice_examples.answer: 3 ('abc'). Use a sliding window with a hash set: expand the right pointer, and when a duplicate is found, shrink from the left until the duplicate is removed. Track the maximum window size.

practice_examples.question: Why should you use ''.join(parts) instead of result += char when building a string in a loop in Python?
practice_examples.answer: Because strings are immutable in Python, += creates a new string object each iteration—O(n²) total. join() allocates once after collecting all parts—O(n) total.

name: Linked Lists
description: A linear data structure of nodes where each node stores a value and a pointer to the next (and optionally previous) node, with no requirement for contiguous memory.
intuition: Each element knows only its own value and who comes next. There is no central index; you must always start from the head and follow the chain.
analogy: A treasure hunt where each clue tells you only the location of the next clue. You cannot teleport to clue 7—you must follow every clue in sequence from the start.
common_misconceptions: Linked lists are always better than arrays for insertion—insertion is O(1) only if you already hold a pointer to the insertion point; finding that point is O(n).
Linked lists use less memory—each node carries pointer overhead absent in arrays.
Traversal speed is comparable to arrays—pointer chasing causes cache misses, making linked lists significantly slower in practice.
key_takeaways: O(1) insertion/deletion given a node pointer; O(n) search.
No random access—must traverse from head.
Singly vs. doubly linked: doubly enables O(1) backward traversal at the cost of an extra pointer.
Classic patterns: Floyd's cycle detection, reversal in-place, merge of two sorted lists.
learning_objectives: Trace pointer manipulation for insert and delete operations on a singly linked list.
Implement in-place reversal of a linked list.
Apply Floyd's two-pointer algorithm to detect a cycle.
Merge two sorted linked lists into one sorted list.

practice_examples.question: Reverse a singly linked list 1→2→3→4→5 in-place and return the new head.
practice_examples.answer: Use three pointers: prev=null, curr=head, next=null. At each step: save next=curr.next, point curr.next=prev, advance prev=curr, curr=next. After the loop prev is the new head (5→4→3→2→1).

practice_examples.question: Given a linked list, determine if it contains a cycle using O(1) extra space.
practice_examples.answer: Floyd's cycle detection: use two pointers, slow (moves 1 step) and fast (moves 2 steps). If they ever meet, a cycle exists. If fast reaches null, no cycle. Time O(n), space O(1).

practice_examples.question: Find the node at the start of a cycle in a linked list if one exists.
practice_examples.answer: After Floyd's detection finds the meeting point, reset one pointer to head. Move both pointers one step at a time. Where they meet again is the cycle start. This follows from the mathematical proof that the distances are congruent.

name: Stacks
description: A LIFO (Last-In, First-Out) abstract data type supporting push, pop, and peek in O(1) time, implemented via arrays or linked lists.
intuition: Only the top of the stack is ever accessible. Adding and removing both happen at the same end, which makes stacks perfect for tracking 'the most recent unresolved thing'.
analogy: A stack of plates in a cafeteria. You place a new plate on top and always take from the top. The plate at the bottom may never be touched until everything above it is removed.
common_misconceptions: Stacks can only be used for undo/redo—they also power call stacks, expression parsing, DFS, backtracking, and monotonic-stack problems.
Stack overflow is a data structure failure—it is a memory exhaustion error from unbounded recursion, not a stack ADT bug.
Pop from an empty stack is safe—this raises an underflow error; always check isEmpty before popping.
key_takeaways: All core operations (push, pop, peek) are O(1).
The call stack in every program is itself a stack.
Monotonic stacks solve next-greater-element class problems in O(n).
Iterative DFS replaces the implicit recursion stack with an explicit one.
learning_objectives: Explain the LIFO property and identify real-world systems that use it.
Implement a stack using an array and using a linked list.
Use a stack to validate balanced parentheses.
Apply a monotonic stack to solve next-greater-element in O(n).

practice_examples.question: Given the string '({[]})' determine if the brackets are valid and balanced.
practice_examples.answer: Use a stack. For each character: if it is an opening bracket push it; if it is a closing bracket, check that the stack top is the matching opener—if not, return false. At the end, return true only if the stack is empty.

practice_examples.question: Find the next greater element for each element in [2, 1, 2, 4, 3]. Output -1 if none exists.
practice_examples.answer: Result: [4, 2, 4, -1, -1]. Use a monotonic decreasing stack. Iterate left to right; when current element > stack top, pop and record current as the answer for the popped element. Push current. Remaining elements in stack get -1.

practice_examples.question: Design a MinStack that supports push, pop, top, and getMin in O(1) time.
practice_examples.answer: Maintain two stacks: the main stack and an auxiliary min-stack. On push(x), also push min(x, min_stack.top()) to the min-stack. On pop, pop both. getMin returns min_stack.top(). All operations remain O(1).

name: Queues
description: A FIFO (First-In, First-Out) abstract data type supporting enqueue and dequeue in O(1) time, with variants including deque, circular buffer, and priority queue.
intuition: Elements are processed in the exact order they arrive. The first element inserted is the first to leave. Nothing skips the line.
analogy: A supermarket checkout queue. Customers join at the back and leave from the front. No matter how long you have been waiting, the only fair exit is from the front.
common_misconceptions: A queue and a stack are interchangeable—the order guarantee is opposite; mixing them produces incorrect BFS/DFS behaviour.
Array-based queues are trivially efficient—naïve array queues have O(n) dequeue; a circular buffer or deque is needed for O(1).
Priority queues are a type of queue—they break FIFO in favour of priority ordering, making them a distinct structure built on heaps.
key_takeaways: O(1) enqueue and dequeue when implemented correctly.
BFS requires a queue; substituting a stack produces DFS.
Circular buffers give O(1) operations with fixed memory.
Deques (double-ended queues) generalise both stacks and queues.
learning_objectives: Explain the FIFO property and contrast it with LIFO.
Implement a queue using a circular buffer to achieve O(1) dequeue.
Use a queue as the backing structure for BFS.
Identify problems best solved with a deque (sliding window maximum).

practice_examples.question: Implement a queue using two stacks such that enqueue is O(1) and dequeue is amortised O(1).
practice_examples.answer: Use stack_in for enqueue (always push to stack_in). For dequeue, if stack_out is empty, pour all elements from stack_in into stack_out (reversing order), then pop from stack_out. Each element moves at most twice, so amortised O(1) per operation.

practice_examples.question: Given a stream of integers and window size k=3, find the maximum in each sliding window of [1,3,-1,-3,5,3,6,7].
practice_examples.answer: Output: [3,3,5,5,6,7]. Use a monotonic deque storing indices. For each element: remove indices outside the window from the front; remove all indices from the back whose values are smaller than current. Front of deque is always the window maximum.

practice_examples.question: Why does a naïve array-backed queue with front-deletion have O(n) dequeue, and how does a circular buffer fix this?
practice_examples.answer: Naïve deletion from the front shifts all remaining elements. A circular buffer maintains head and tail pointers; dequeue just increments head mod capacity—O(1) with no shifting.

name: Recursion
description: A problem-solving technique where a function calls itself with a smaller sub-problem until it reaches a base case, relying on the call stack to compose results on the way back.
intuition: Break the problem into a smaller version of itself plus a trivial extra step. Trust that the recursive call correctly solves the smaller version, then combine.
analogy: Looking up a word in a dictionary that defines it using another word you don't know. You look up that word too, and the next, until you find a word you already know—then you unwind your understanding back to the original.
common_misconceptions: Recursion is always elegant and efficient—without memoisation, naive recursion can have exponential time complexity (e.g., Fibonacci).
Every recursive solution needs recursion—all recursion can be converted to iteration with an explicit stack.
The base case is optional—omitting it causes infinite recursion and a stack-overflow crash.
Tail recursion is automatically optimised—only specific languages and compilers perform tail-call optimisation.
key_takeaways: Every recursive function needs: a base case, a recursive case, and progress toward the base case.
Space complexity includes O(depth) stack frames.
Divide-and-conquer, tree traversals, and backtracking are naturally recursive paradigms.
Memoisation converts exponential recursion to polynomial time.
learning_objectives: Identify the base case and recursive case in any recursive function.
Trace the call stack for a simple recursive function (factorial, Fibonacci).
Explain why naive recursive Fibonacci is O(2^n) and how memoisation reduces it to O(n).
Convert a recursive solution to an iterative one using an explicit stack.
Apply recursion to divide-and-conquer problems (merge sort, binary search).

practice_examples.question: Write a recursive function to compute the nth Fibonacci number. What is its time complexity without memoisation?
practice_examples.answer: fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2), base cases fib(0)=0, fib(1)=1. Without memoisation, time complexity is O(2^n) because each call branches into two, creating an exponential call tree with heavy recomputation.

practice_examples.question: Given an array, use recursion to find all subsets (the power set).
practice_examples.answer: At each index, make two recursive calls: one including the current element, one excluding it. Base case: when index equals array length, record the current subset. This produces all 2^n subsets. Time O(2^n), space O(n) for the recursion stack.

practice_examples.question: Explain the difference between head recursion and tail recursion, and why it matters.
practice_examples.answer: Head recursion: the recursive call happens before any work in the current frame (result depends on return value). Tail recursion: the recursive call is the very last action; no pending computation remains. Tail recursion can be optimised by compilers into a loop (O(1) stack space vs O(n) for head recursion).

name: Trees
description: A hierarchical, acyclic connected graph of nodes where one node is the root, every other node has exactly one parent, and nodes with no children are leaves.
intuition: A tree imposes a parent-child hierarchy on data. Every node except the root has exactly one parent, enabling efficient hierarchical decomposition and recursive processing.
analogy: A company org chart. The CEO is the root. Every employee reports to exactly one manager. Departments nest inside divisions. There are no cycles—an employee cannot be their own manager's manager.
common_misconceptions: A tree is just a special graph—conceptually true, but tree algorithms exploit the no-cycle and single-parent guarantees to achieve O(log n) heights that general graphs lack.
Height and depth are the same—height is measured from a node down to the deepest leaf; depth is measured from the root down to the node.
Binary trees are always balanced—an unbalanced binary tree degrades to O(n) in the worst case.
key_takeaways: n nodes → exactly n−1 edges.
Traversal orders: in-order, pre-order, post-order (DFS variants), and level-order (BFS).
Height determines worst-case operation time for search trees.
Recursive tree algorithms are natural: solve left subtree, solve right subtree, combine.
learning_objectives: Distinguish between tree height, depth, and level.
Implement pre-order, in-order, and post-order traversals both recursively and iteratively.
Compute the height of a binary tree using recursion.
Identify which traversal order to use for a given problem (e.g., in-order for sorted output).

practice_examples.question: Given a binary tree, compute its maximum depth (number of nodes along the longest root-to-leaf path).
practice_examples.answer: maxDepth(node) = 0 if node is null, else 1 + max(maxDepth(node.left), maxDepth(node.right)). Time O(n), space O(h) where h is the height.

practice_examples.question: Return the level-order traversal of a binary tree as a list of lists (one list per level).
practice_examples.answer: BFS with a queue. At each level, record the queue's current size, process exactly that many nodes (capturing their values), then enqueue their children. Each iteration of the outer loop corresponds to one level.

practice_examples.question: In what scenario does post-order traversal produce the correct result but pre-order produces the wrong one? Give an example problem.
practice_examples.answer: Deleting a tree: you must delete children before deleting the parent (post-order). With pre-order, deleting the root first loses access to the children. Similarly, computing subtree sizes requires knowing child sizes before computing the parent's size—post-order is mandatory.

name: Binary Search Tree
description: A binary tree where every node's left subtree contains only values less than the node and the right subtree contains only values greater, enabling O(log n) average-case search, insertion, and deletion.
intuition: The BST property lets you make a binary decision at every node: go left if smaller, right if larger. Each comparison halves the remaining search space—identical reasoning to binary search on a sorted array.
analogy: A library where every book section has a sign: 'books with earlier titles to the left, later titles to the right.' You navigate to any book by reading only the signs at each junction without checking every shelf.
common_misconceptions: BST operations are always O(log n)—only if the tree is balanced; a sorted-insertion sequence produces a O(n) height degenerate tree.
In-order BST traversal produces unsorted output—it produces exactly sorted ascending output.
BSTs and heaps are interchangeable—heaps are complete binary trees optimised for min/max; BSTs support ordered traversal and range queries.
key_takeaways: Average O(log n) for search, insert, delete; worst case O(n) when unbalanced.
In-order traversal yields sorted sequence—useful for k-th smallest/largest.
Self-balancing variants (AVL, Red-Black) guarantee O(log n) worst case.
Predecessor/successor queries are natural BST operations.
learning_objectives: Verify whether a given binary tree satisfies the BST property.
Implement search, insert, and delete in a BST.
Explain why inserting sorted data into a BST degrades performance to O(n).
Use in-order traversal to retrieve BST elements in sorted order.
Find the k-th smallest element in a BST.

practice_examples.question: Validate whether a binary tree is a valid BST. The tree has nodes [5, 1, 4, null, null, 3, 6].
practice_examples.answer: This is NOT a valid BST. Node 4 is in the right subtree of 5 but 4 < 5—violating the BST property. The correct check passes min/max bounds recursively: every node in the right subtree must be strictly greater than its ancestor that branched right.

practice_examples.question: Find the kth smallest element in a BST without extra space beyond the recursion stack.
practice_examples.answer: Perform in-order traversal (left → node → right), which visits nodes in ascending sorted order. Decrement a counter at each visit; when the counter reaches 0, return the current node's value. Time O(h + k), space O(h).

practice_examples.question: Insert values [5, 3, 7, 1, 4] into an empty BST in that order. Draw the resulting tree. What is its height?
practice_examples.answer: 5 is root. 3 goes left of 5. 7 goes right of 5. 1 goes left of 3. 4 goes right of 3. Height = 2 (three levels, height counted as edges from root to deepest leaf). The tree is reasonably balanced for this input sequence.

name: Heap
description: A complete binary tree satisfying the heap property—every parent is greater (max-heap) or smaller (min-heap) than its children—enabling O(1) peek and O(log n) insert/extract of the extremum.
intuition: A heap makes a single guarantee: the top is always the best element (largest or smallest). It does not sort the rest. That limited promise is exactly what makes it efficient.
analogy: A tournament bracket where the champion is always at the top. When the champion leaves, the best remaining player bubbles up. You never see the full ranked order—only who wins next.
common_misconceptions: A heap is always a tree in memory—heaps are typically stored as arrays using index arithmetic (left child = 2i+1, right child = 2i+2).
Heapify is O(n log n)—building a heap from an array (bottom-up heapify) is O(n).
A heap supports O(log n) arbitrary search—it does not; only the root is directly accessible, arbitrary search is O(n).
Min-heap and priority queue are the same thing—a priority queue is an ADT; a heap is its most common implementation.
key_takeaways: O(1) peek-min/max; O(log n) insert and extract.
Heap sort is in-place O(n log n) with O(1) extra space.
The heap is the canonical data structure for scheduling, Dijkstra's algorithm, and k-way merge problems.
Building a heap from n elements is O(n), not O(n log n).
learning_objectives: Map a heap stored as an array to its logical tree representation using index formulas.
Trace the sift-up and sift-down operations after insert and extract-min/max.
Explain why building a heap bottom-up is O(n) rather than O(n log n).
Apply a heap to find the k largest elements in a stream.

practice_examples.question: Find the k=3 largest elements from the stream [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6] using a heap with O(n log k) time.
practice_examples.answer: Use a min-heap of size k. For each element: push it onto the heap; if the heap size exceeds k, pop the minimum. After processing all elements, the heap contains the k largest. Result: {5, 6, 9}.

practice_examples.question: Given a min-heap stored as array [1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 8], insert the value 2. Show the resulting array.
practice_examples.answer: Append 2 at index 6 → [1,3,5,7,9,8,2]. Sift up: parent of index 6 is index 2 (value 5). 2 < 5 so swap → [1,3,2,7,9,8,5]. Parent of index 2 is index 0 (value 1). 2 > 1, stop. Final: [1,3,2,7,9,8,5].

practice_examples.question: Merge k sorted arrays each of size n using a heap. What is the time complexity?
practice_examples.answer: O(n·k · log k). Initialise a min-heap with the first element from each of the k arrays (with source tracking). Each extract-min is O(log k); after extraction, push the next element from the same array. Total elements = n·k, each requiring O(log k) heap operations.

name: Depth-First Search
description: A graph/tree traversal algorithm that explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking, using a stack (implicit via recursion or explicit) and visited tracking.
intuition: Commit fully to one path. Go as deep as possible. Only when you hit a dead end do you back up and try the next unexplored branch. You fully exhaust one avenue before widening.
analogy: Exploring a cave system. You always take the first unexplored tunnel you see, going deeper and deeper. When you reach a dead end or a place you've been, you backtrack to the last fork and try the next passage.
common_misconceptions: DFS always finds the shortest path—it does not; BFS finds shortest path in unweighted graphs.
DFS cannot work on graphs with cycles—it can with a visited set to prevent revisiting.
DFS and BFS have different time complexities—both are O(V+E) on graphs.
key_takeaways: Time: O(V+E); Space: O(V) for the visited set and recursion stack.
Natural for: topological sort, cycle detection, connected components, path existence.
Pre-order, in-order, post-order tree traversals are all DFS variants.
Iterative DFS with an explicit stack reverses the visitation order compared to recursive DFS unless node ordering is adjusted.
learning_objectives: Trace DFS execution order on a graph and record the visited sequence.
Implement DFS both recursively and iteratively with an explicit stack.
Use DFS to detect cycles in a directed graph using recursion-stack colouring.
Apply DFS to compute connected components in an undirected graph.
Perform topological sort using DFS post-order.

practice_examples.question: Given an undirected graph with edges {0-1, 0-2, 1-3, 2-4}, list the DFS traversal order starting from node 0 (visit lower-numbered neighbours first).
practice_examples.answer: 0 → 1 → 3 → 2 → 4. From 0, visit 1 first (lower number). From 1, visit 3 (only unvisited neighbour). Backtrack to 0, visit 2. From 2, visit 4.

practice_examples.question: Detect whether a directed graph with edges {0→1, 1→2, 2→0, 3→4} contains a cycle.
practice_examples.answer: Yes, there is a cycle: 0→1→2→0. Use DFS with three states per node: unvisited (white), in current path (gray), fully explored (black). If DFS reaches a gray node, a back edge (cycle) is found. Node 2 reaches gray node 0.

practice_examples.question: Count the number of islands in a 2D grid where '1' is land and '0' is water.
practice_examples.answer: Iterate over every cell. When a '1' is found, increment the island count and run DFS to mark all connected '1's as visited (set to '0' or use a visited array). DFS explores 4-directional neighbours. Time O(m·n), space O(m·n) for the recursion stack.

name: Breadth-First Search
description: A graph/tree traversal algorithm that explores all neighbours at the present depth before moving to nodes at the next depth level, using a queue to guarantee level-order processing.
intuition: Expand outward in concentric rings. Visit all immediate neighbours before visiting anyone further away. This layered exploration guarantees that the first time you reach a node, you have found the shortest unweighted path.
analogy: Ripples in a pond. Drop a stone and the first ring of water reaches nearby points, then the second ring reaches points further out. Every point at distance d is touched before any point at distance d+1.
common_misconceptions: BFS works only on trees—it works on any graph; a visited set handles cycles.
BFS finds the shortest path in weighted graphs—it finds fewest-edges paths; Dijkstra handles weighted shortest paths.
BFS uses more memory than DFS—in wide shallow graphs BFS memory (entire frontier) can dwarf DFS stack depth.
key_takeaways: Time: O(V+E); Space: O(V) for the queue and visited set.
Guarantees shortest path (minimum edges) in unweighted graphs.
Level-order tree traversal is BFS.
Multi-source BFS starts from multiple nodes simultaneously, useful for 'nearest' problems.
learning_objectives: Trace BFS execution layer by layer on a graph and record shortest distances from the source.
Implement BFS using a queue with a visited set.
Prove why BFS guarantees minimum-edge shortest path in unweighted graphs.
Apply multi-source BFS to find the nearest target for all cells in a grid.

practice_examples.question: Find the shortest path (in number of edges) from node 0 to node 4 in an undirected graph with edges {0-1, 0-2, 1-3, 2-4, 3-4}.
practice_examples.answer: Shortest path is 2 edges: 0→2→4. BFS from node 0 visits level 1: {1,2}, level 2: {3,4}. Node 4 is first reached at level 2, so distance = 2.

practice_examples.question: In a binary matrix, find the shortest path from top-left (0,0) to bottom-right (n-1,n-1) where 0 is open and 1 is blocked (8-directional movement).
practice_examples.answer: Multi-directional BFS from (0,0). Enqueue start, mark visited, track distance. For each cell dequeued, explore all 8 neighbours that are in-bounds, unblocked, and unvisited. Return the distance when (n-1,n-1) is dequeued. Return -1 if queue empties. Time O(n²).

practice_examples.question: Given a grid of 0s and 1s where 1s are gates and 0s are empty rooms, fill each empty room with its distance to the nearest gate using multi-source BFS.
practice_examples.answer: Initialise the BFS queue with all gate positions simultaneously (multi-source). Process level by level: for each cell dequeued, attempt to fill adjacent empty rooms with current_distance + 1 and enqueue them. Because all gates start at distance 0 simultaneously, the first time any room is reached gives the minimum gate distance. Time O(m·n).

name: Graphs
description: A set of vertices (nodes) connected by edges (directed or undirected, weighted or unweighted), representing pairwise relationships without the structural constraints of trees.
intuition: A graph is the most general relational structure. Unlike trees, any node can connect to any other, edges can have direction and weight, and cycles are allowed—making graphs the right model for roads, networks, dependencies, and social connections.
analogy: A city road map. Intersections are nodes; roads are edges. Some roads are one-way (directed). Each road has a distance (weighted). You can drive in circles (cycles exist). There is no single 'root' intersection.
common_misconceptions: Trees are not graphs—trees are a subset of graphs: connected, acyclic, undirected graphs.
Adjacency matrices and adjacency lists are equivalent in all cases—matrices give O(1) edge lookup but use O(V²) space; lists use O(V+E) space but O(degree) lookup.
Graphs must be connected—many real graphs (e.g., dependency graphs) are disconnected.
key_takeaways: Representations: adjacency matrix O(V²) space; adjacency list O(V+E) space.
Key properties: directed vs. undirected, weighted vs. unweighted, cyclic vs. acyclic, connected vs. disconnected.
Fundamental algorithms: BFS, DFS, Dijkstra, Bellman-Ford, Floyd-Warshall, Kruskal, Prim, topological sort.
Recognising the correct graph model is the first step in solving graph problems.
learning_objectives: Represent a graph as both an adjacency matrix and an adjacency list, and compare trade-offs.
Classify a graph by its properties: directed/undirected, weighted/unweighted, cyclic/acyclic.
Determine which graph algorithm is appropriate given a problem's constraints.
Compute in-degree and out-degree for nodes in a directed graph.

practice_examples.question: Convert the edge list [(0,1), (0,2), (1,3), (2,3)] into an adjacency list and an adjacency matrix for an undirected graph with 4 nodes.
You have a graph with 5 nodes and 4 edges. Is it necessarily a tree? What additional conditions are required?
practice_examples.answer: Not necessarily. A tree with 5 nodes has exactly 4 edges AND is connected AND has no cycles. A graph can have n-1 edges with cycles (if disconnected) or no cycles but be disconnected. All three conditions—n-1 edges, connected, acyclic—must hold for it to be a tree.

practice_examples.question: Given a dense graph with 1000 nodes and ~900,000 edges, which representation is preferable and why?
practice_examples.answer: Adjacency matrix. With ~900,000 edges out of a possible 999,000 (nearly complete graph), the list overhead (storing lists with pointers) approaches the matrix size. The matrix provides O(1) edge existence queries and has negligible waste. Adjacency lists are preferred for sparse graphs where E << V².

name: Hash Tables
description: A data structure that maps keys to values using a hash function to compute an array index, achieving O(1) average-case insert, delete, and lookup at the cost of O(n) worst case with poor hashing.
intuition: Instead of searching through all keys, convert the key into an address using a mathematical transformation. Go directly to that address. The magic (and risk) lives entirely in the quality of the hash function.
analogy: A coat-check room where the attendant uses your name to deterministically assign you a numbered hook. To retrieve your coat, they recompute the same number. No searching required—unless two people get the same hook (collision).
common_misconceptions: Hash tables are always O(1)—worst-case is O(n) with adversarial inputs causing all keys to collide.
Any function can be a hash function—a good hash function must distribute keys uniformly and be deterministic.
Hash tables preserve insertion order—standard hash tables do not; LinkedHashMap/ordered dict variants do.
Collision is rare enough to ignore—load factor management and collision resolution (chaining vs. open addressing) are essential design decisions.
key_takeaways: O(1) average insert, lookup, delete; O(n) worst case.
Load factor (n/capacity) controls performance; rehashing at ~0.75 is standard.
Collision resolution: separate chaining (linked lists per bucket) vs. open addressing (linear/quadratic probing).
Hash sets and hash maps are the most-used data structures in competitive programming for O(1) frequency counting and deduplication.
learning_objectives: Explain how a hash function maps a key to a bucket index.
Describe separate chaining and open addressing as collision resolution strategies.
Calculate load factor and explain when and why rehashing occurs.
Apply a hash map to solve two-sum, frequency counting, and grouping problems in O(n).

practice_examples.question: Given an array [1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3, 4], find all elements that appear more than once.
practice_examples.answer: Use a hash map to count frequencies: iterate once, incrementing each element's count. Then iterate the map and collect keys with count > 1. Result: {1, 2, 3}. Time O(n), space O(n).

practice_examples.question: Group the strings ['eat','tea','tan','ate','nat','bat'] by anagram using a hash map.
practice_examples.answer: For each string, sort its characters to create a canonical key (e.g., 'eat'→'aet'). Use the sorted key to group: {'aet':['eat','tea','ate'], 'ant':['tan','nat'], 'abt':['bat']}. Time O(n · k log k) where k is max string length.

practice_examples.question: A hash table has 8 buckets and uses hash(key) = key % 8. Insert keys [10, 18, 26]. Where does each land, and what collision strategy would you use?
practice_examples.answer: All three keys hash to bucket 2 (10%8=2, 18%8=2, 26%8=2)—all collide. With separate chaining, bucket 2 holds a linked list [10→18→26]. With linear probing, 10 goes to 2, 18 to 3, 26 to 4 (next open slots). This illustrates why uniform key distribution matters.

name: Binary Search
description: A divide-and-conquer search algorithm that locates a target in a sorted structure by repeatedly halving the search interval, achieving O(log n) time.
intuition: Every comparison eliminates half the remaining possibilities. In a sorted space, knowing whether the target is to the left or right of the midpoint is enough to discard the other half permanently.
analogy: Guessing a number between 1 and 1000. You always guess the midpoint. 'Too high'—you now know it's in the lower half. 'Too low'—upper half. Ten guesses suffice for 1024 numbers because 2¹⁰ = 1024.
common_misconceptions: Binary search only finds exact matches—it can find insertion points, first/last occurrences, and the answer to any monotonic predicate.
The array must be sorted in ascending order—descending or custom-ordered arrays work with an adjusted comparison.
The midpoint calculation mid = (lo + hi) / 2 is always safe—it can overflow in languages with fixed-width integers; use lo + (hi - lo) / 2.
Binary search is trivial to implement—off-by-one errors in loop bounds and mid-assignment make it notoriously bug-prone.
key_takeaways: O(log n) time; O(1) space (iterative).
Requires a sorted (or monotonic) search space—the invariant that enables halving.
Binary search on the answer space: when the answer is monotonically feasible/infeasible, binary search on the answer itself.
Three templates: find exact, find leftmost, find rightmost—each has a subtly different loop condition and return value.
learning_objectives: Implement binary search iteratively with correct loop termination and midpoint calculation.
Identify and fix off-by-one errors in binary search boundaries.
Apply binary search to find the first and last occurrence of a target in a sorted array.
Recognise when a problem can be solved by binary searching on the answer rather than the array.

practice_examples.question: Search for target 7 in sorted array [1, 3, 5, 7, 9, 11]. Return its index or -1 if not found.
practice_examples.answer: lo=0, hi=5. mid=2 (value 5), 7>5, lo=3. mid=4 (value 9), 7<9, hi=3. mid=3 (value 7), match—return index 3. Terminates in 3 comparisons vs 4 linear.

practice_examples.question: Find the leftmost (first) index of target 5 in [1, 3, 5, 5, 5, 7, 9].
practice_examples.answer: Use the leftmost binary search template: when nums[mid] == target, don't return—record mid as a candidate and set hi = mid - 1 to continue searching left. Final answer is the last recorded candidate. Result: index 2.

practice_examples.question: Given that a factory can produce m bouquets from n flower bloom days, and you need exactly m bouquets of k consecutive flowers, find the minimum number of days to wait. Bloom days: [1,10,3,10,2], m=3, k=1.
practice_examples.answer: Binary search on the answer (number of days). The feasibility function canMake(days, d) checks if waiting d days yields enough bloomed flowers to form m bouquets of k consecutive. This is monotonic: if d days work, d+1 also works. Binary search the range [min(bloom), max(bloom)]. For this input, answer is 3.

name: Greedy Algorithms
description: An algorithmic paradigm that builds a solution incrementally by always making the locally optimal choice at each step, without reconsidering past decisions, hoping (and provably achieving for certain problems) a global optimum.
intuition: At each step, take the best available option right now and never look back. This works when the problem has the greedy-choice property: a local optimum at each step leads to a global optimum.
analogy: Making change with the fewest coins in a standard currency. Always take the largest coin that fits. For US denominations this always works. For arbitrary denominations it fails—demonstrating that greedy correctness must be proved, not assumed.
common_misconceptions: Greedy algorithms always produce the optimal solution—they do only when the greedy-choice property and optimal substructure hold; otherwise use DP.
Greedy and DP are the same—DP considers all subproblem solutions; greedy commits to one choice per step without exploration.
Proving greedy correctness is optional—without proof (usually by exchange argument or induction), a greedy solution is a heuristic, not an algorithm.
key_takeaways: Must prove two properties: greedy-choice property and optimal substructure.
Classic greedy problems: activity selection, interval scheduling, Huffman coding, Dijkstra, Kruskal/Prim MST, fractional knapsack.
When greedy fails (0/1 knapsack), dynamic programming is typically the correct paradigm.
Greedy algorithms are usually O(n log n) due to sorting as a preprocessing step.
learning_objectives: Define the greedy-choice property and optimal substructure for a given problem.
Prove greedy correctness using an exchange argument.
Apply greedy to interval scheduling: select maximum non-overlapping intervals.
Distinguish problems where greedy is optimal from those requiring DP (fractional vs 0/1 knapsack).
Implement Huffman coding as a greedy tree-building algorithm.

practice_examples.question: Given intervals [(1,3),(2,4),(3,5),(4,6)], find the maximum number of non-overlapping intervals.
practice_examples.answer: Sort by end time. Greedily select an interval if its start >= last selected end. Sort: [(1,3),(2,4),(3,5),(4,6)]. Select (1,3). (2,4) starts at 2 < 3, skip. (3,5) starts at 3 >= 3, select. (4,6) starts at 4 < 5, skip. Max = 2 intervals.

practice_examples.question: You have item weights [2,3,5] and values [3,4,5] with a knapsack capacity of 5. Compare greedy (by value/weight ratio) vs the optimal solution.
practice_examples.answer: Ratios: item1=1.5, item2=1.33, item3=1.0. Greedy takes item1 (weight 2, value 3), then item2 partially (weight 3, value 4)—valid for fractional. For 0/1 knapsack, optimal is item2+item1 = value 7 if both fit, or just item3 = value 5. Greedy gives wrong answer for 0/1 version; DP is required.

practice_examples.question: Prove that the greedy algorithm for activity selection (pick the activity with the earliest finish time) is optimal.
practice_examples.answer: Exchange argument: suppose the greedy solution G differs from optimal O. Let a be the first activity in G not in O. Swap the activity b in O that conflicts with a for a itself. Since a finishes no later than b (greedy picks earliest finish), all subsequent activities in O remain compatible. The swapped solution has the same size as O, showing G is also optimal.

name: Dynamic Programming
description: An optimisation paradigm that solves complex problems by breaking them into overlapping subproblems, solving each subproblem once, and storing results (memoisation or tabulation) to avoid redundant computation.
intuition: If you solve the same smaller problem repeatedly, solve it once and remember the answer. DP is systematic memoisation applied to problems with optimal substructure—the optimal solution to the whole is built from optimal solutions to its parts.
analogy: Computing Fibonacci numbers by writing down every value as you go rather than recomputing from scratch each time. Once you know F(10), you never recompute it—you look it up. DP scales this caching discipline to complex multidimensional state spaces.
common_misconceptions: DP always uses a 2D table—state dimensionality depends on the problem; many DP problems use 1D, 3D, or even higher-dimensional state.
Memoisation (top-down) and tabulation (bottom-up) always perform identically—tabulation avoids recursion overhead and can be more cache-friendly; memoisation avoids computing unnecessary states.
DP solves all optimisation problems—it requires optimal substructure; problems where subproblem solutions invalidate each other cannot use DP.

Identifying DP is straightforward—recognising DP applicability (vs. greedy) is one of the hardest interview skills; look for: overlapping subproblems, optimal substructure, and a question asking for count/min/max.
key_takeaways: Two conditions for DP: overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure.
Top-down (memoisation): recursive + cache. Bottom-up (tabulation): iterative, fill table in dependency order.
State definition is the core skill—correctly defining what each cell represents determines correctness.
Space optimisation: many DP tables can be compressed to O(1) or O(n) by keeping only the previous row/column.
Classic problems: 0/1 knapsack, longest common subsequence, edit distance, coin change, matrix chain multiplication, longest increasing subsequence.
learning_objectives: Identify overlapping subproblems and optimal substructure in a new problem.
Define the state, recurrence relation, and base cases for a DP problem.
Implement both top-down (memoisation) and bottom-up (tabulation) solutions for the same problem.
Optimise a 2D DP table to use O(n) or O(1) space by keeping only necessary rows.
Recognise the DP pattern: count ways, minimum cost, maximum value, or feasibility.

practice_examples.question: Find the minimum number of coins to make amount=11 using coins=[1,5,6,9].
practice_examples.answer: DP table dp[0..11], dp[0]=0, all others=infinity. For each amount a from 1 to 11, try each coin c: dp[a] = min(dp[a], dp[a-c]+1). Result: dp[11]=2 (coins 5+6). Note: greedy gives 9+1+1=3 coins—wrong. DP is required because greedy fails here.

practice_examples.question: Find the length of the Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) of 'ABCBDAB' and 'BDCAB'.
practice_examples.answer: Build a 2D dp table where dp[i][j] = LCS length of first i chars of s1 and first j chars of s2. If s1[i-1]==s2[j-1]: dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]+1. Else: dp[i][j] = max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). LCS length = dp[7][5] = 4 (e.g., 'BCAB' or 'BDAB').

practice_examples.question: Count the number of ways to climb n=4 stairs, taking 1 or 2 steps at a time.
practice_examples.answer: dp[0]=1 (base), dp[1]=1, dp[i]=dp[i-1]+dp[i-2]. dp[2]=2, dp[3]=3, dp[4]=5. This is the Fibonacci pattern: at each stair, you either came from one step below or two steps below, and those cases don't overlap, so they sum directly.
